sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Graphic shows the skull of a newborn, including the anterior fontanelle, coronal, metopic, and sagittal sutures. Cephalohematoma is subperiosteal, focal, and limited by sutures. Subgaleal hematoma is under the scalp aponeurosis, much more extensive, and not bounded by sutures.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-01

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Bone CT in a newborn with a traumatic delivery shows a linear skull fracture and a cephalohematoma overlying the parietal bone. Note that the cephalohematoma does not cross the sagittal suture .
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-02

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: NECT in the same patient shows the cephalohematoma . Note the presence of a small intracranial epidural hematoma .
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-03

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT shows an infant with a large subgaleal hematoma. In contrast to cephalohematoma, this subgaleal hematoma crosses the midline and extends nearly all the way around the entire head. Large subgaleal hematomas in infants can cause life-threatening blood loss.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-04

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Autopsied skull shows fatal trauma with exo- (L) & endocranial views (R). A linear fracture extends into the superior sagittal suture , causing diastasis & a subgaleal hematoma that the crosses midline.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-05

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Coronal NECT shows a subgaleal hematoma crossing the midline sagittal suture. Skull base & facial fractures & a left frontal skull fracture are better seen on bone windows. Note the pneumocephalus , indicating a fracture through a paranasal sinus through a paranasal sinus or the calvarium.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-06

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial bone CT in a 48-year-old trauma patient shows a left temporal bone skull fracture that also involves the greater wing of the sphenoid .
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-07

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT in the same patient shows an associated epidural hematoma in the anterior middle cranial fossa. Skull fractures are commonly associated with epidural hematomas. Note the contrecoup subarachnoid hemorrhage in the right temporal region.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-08

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT in a patient with a "growing" skull fracture shows a widened fracture with scalloped margins .
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-09

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial T2 MR in the same patient shows a lobulated CSF collection filling the "growing" fracture. CSF pulsations have widened and scalloped the calvarium. "Growing" skull fractures are difficult to detect in the acute stage and are often identified on follow-up imaging as a progressively widening, unhealing fracture. On CT, they are lucent lesions with rounded, scalloped margins.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-10

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT in a newborn with difficult vacuum-assisted delivery shows a small right and a large left cephalohematoma. Neither crosses the midline.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-11

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Coronal NECT in the same patient shows the small right and large left cephalohematomas. The elevated periosteum clearly separates the two blood collections.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-12

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Sagittal NECT shows the left parietal cephalohematoma does not cross the coronal suture .
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-13

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial T2 MR in an abused infant shows a very large mixed acute/subacute subgaleal hematoma crossing the sutures and extending over the face/orbits.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-14

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: More cephalad axial T2 MR shows the mixed-age subgaleal hematoma crossing the midline.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-15

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT (left) shows severe scalp laceration with a combination of elevated and depressed skull fractures. Axial bone CT (right) shows that the elevated fracture is literally "hinged" away from the calvarium.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-16

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT shows a large subgaleal hematoma crossing the sagittal suture. Hyperdense vertex hematoma also crosses the midline, suggesting that the blood is in the epidural space.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-17

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial bone CT shows a diastatic fracture of the sagittal suture . A very large subgaleal hematoma extends around the entire circumference of the skull. The superior sagittal sinus is torn, and the intracranial blood seen on soft tissue windows was a vertex venous epidural hematoma.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-18

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT in a patient with a blow to the right side of the head shows a mildly depressed fracture . Note that the brain under the fracture appears quite normal.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-19

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT with bone algorithm reconstruction and wide window display in the same patient shows a depressed, comminuted skull fracture involving the right squamous temporal bone. Note the subcutaneous air and soft tissue hematoma overlying the fracture.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-20

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT in a patient who was hit in the head shows an extensively comminuted, depressed skull fracture . Note the large subgaleal hematoma completely surrounding the entire skull.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-21

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Bone CT through the top of the calvarium in a patient with severe head injury shows linear skull fractures extending into and widening the sagittal suture, causing a diastatic fracture .
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-22

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial NECT [brain (L) and bone (R) windows] in a young male who was hit with a hammer shows a comminuted, depressed skull fracture . The brain under the fracture shows only a small cortical contusion . The patient's GCS was 15.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-23

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial graphic depicts different basilar skull fractures crossing the petrous apex and clivus as well as extending into the jugular foramen and carotid canal . The potential for vascular injury in such fractures is high.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-24

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: 3D shaded surface display in a patient with multiple linear and diastatic skull fractures shows utility of SSDs in depicting complex fracture anatomy. Note slight depression of the fractured parietooccipital calvarium that was appreciated only on this SSD.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-25

sourceQualifiedImages.caption: Axial bone CT shows a subgaleal hematoma crossing the midline sagittal suture. A left frontal skull fracture is present. Note the pneumocephalus . Subgaleal hematomas are under the scalp aponeurosis, much more extensive, and not bounded by sutures. They are often associated with skull fractures in trauma patients.
sourceQualifiedImages.label: SDX-26

downloadSentinel:
[Download the TSV](sandbox:/mnt/data/radprimer_cards.tsv)

CORE_EVIDENCE_FILE_BEGIN
CORE_EVIDENCE_STATUS: USED
CORE_SOURCE_BASIS: Core Radiology, Second Edition; uploaded file junzi-shi-core-radiology-a-visual-approach-to.pdf; Pediatric Imaging / Child abuse - non-accidental trauma, Peds pp. 1165-1166; Spine Imaging / Imaging in cervical spine trauma and occipital condyle fractures, Spine pp. 899-900.
CORE_FACTS_USED:

* Complex skull fractures that are depressed, multiple, bilateral, or crossing sutures are suspicious for child abuse.
* Isolated linear skull fracture is nonspecific and can be seen in both abuse and accidental trauma.
* Bone scintigraphy is insensitive for skull fractures.
* CT is preferred for trauma fracture assessment when imaging is indicated; MRI is not the initial modality for fracture detection but can help with selected complications/soft-tissue or ligament questions.
CORE_DERIVED_CARDS:
* Epidemiology/etiology card: isolated linear skull fracture is nonspecific for child abuse.
* High-yield card: complex skull fracture patterns suspicious for child abuse.
* High-yield card: bone scintigraphy limitation for skull fractures.
* Summary field Core Cross-Check section.
CORE_LIMITATIONS:
* Core did not provide a dedicated Scalp and Skull Injuries article matching the RadPrimer/STATdx source package; Core content was used only as cross-checking support for pediatric skull fracture abuse specificity and general trauma imaging modality context.
CORE_EVIDENCE_FILE_END

RADPRIMER_CARD_TSV_DOWNLOAD_READY
FILENAME: radprimer_cards.tsv
EXPECTED_ROWS: 36
